blocks.content: That was never a good sign.

If her alarm woke her, she could be dramatic about it. Groan into her pillow. Accuse the universe of cruelty. Bargain for five more minutes like time was a customer service representative who might be persuaded.
But waking before the alarm meant her body had gotten there first.
She opened her eyes to the pale ceiling and stayed still.
For a few seconds, she listened.
Her room was quiet. The house was quiet. Somewhere beyond her window, a bird decided morning was worth announcing and began doing so with offensive confidence.
She turned onto her side and saw Winston sitting crookedly against her pillow, one plastic eye higher than the other, looking deeply unqualified to manage anyone's emotional well-being.
She sat up carefully, pausing only when the familiar tightness flickered beneath her ribs.
Cassandra breathed in, slow and measured.
She smiled as if she had won something.
Small victories counted. Anyone who said otherwise had clearly never needed them.
